eli3: REST API serving exposes ML models via HTTP endpoints, just like any web service. Client sends a POST request with input data, server returns predictions in JSON. Simple, universal, and easy to integrate.
Think of it as a restaurant: you (client) order a dish (prediction request), the kitchen (model) prepares it, and you receive the meal (response). REST is the standard way to order and receive.

Tradeoffs: HTTP overhead adds latency (tens to hundreds of milliseconds), but benefits are huge — language-agnostic, firewall-friendly, well-understood by developers, and easy to load balance and cache.

whenWhy.title: Web and mobile applications
whenWhy.body: Most common pattern. Mobile app sends user context to /predict endpoint, receives recommendation or classification. Works with any HTTP client. Flask, FastAPI, and Express popular for wrapping models in REST APIs.

whenWhy.title: Microservices architecture
whenWhy.body: ML model as one service in a larger system. Other services call it via REST. Loose coupling, independent scaling, language flexibility. Kubernetes ingress routes requests, services discover each other via DNS.

whenWhy.title: Third-party API integrations
whenWhy.body: Exposing predictions to external customers or partners. REST is universally understood. Authentication via API keys, OAuth. Rate limiting prevents abuse. Documentation via OpenAPI/Swagger.

whenWhy.title: Proof of concept and prototyping
whenWhy.body: Fastest way to deploy a model for testing. Wrap sklearn/TensorFlow/PyTorch model in Flask app, deploy to Heroku/EC2. Validate business value before investing in optimized serving infrastructure.

deepDiveIntro: REST API design for ML: (1) Endpoint: POST /predict or /v1/models/{model}/predict. (2) Request format: JSON with input features or raw data. (3) Response format: predictions, confidence scores, model version, request_id for tracing. (4) Headers: Content-Type, Authorization, X-Request-ID. (5) Error handling: proper HTTP status codes (400 for bad input, 500 for model error), structured error messages.

Implementation frameworks: Python (FastAPI, Flask, Django REST), Go (Gin, Echo), Java (Spring Boot, Dropwizard). FastAPI popular for ML: async support, automatic OpenAPI docs, pydantic validation. Containerize with Docker, orchestrate with Kubernetes. Horizontal scaling via replica sets and load balancers.

deepDive.heading: API Design Patterns
deepDive.body: Single prediction: POST /predict with {features: [...]}. Batch prediction: POST /predict_batch with {instances: [[...], [...]]}. Model versioning: /v1/predict, /v2/predict for A/B testing. Health check: GET /health returns model status, readiness. Metrics: GET /metrics for Prometheus scraping.

deepDive.heading: Request/Response Schema
deepDive.body: Request: {instances: [...], context: {...}}. Response: {predictions: [...], confidence: [...], model_version: "x.y.z", request_id: "uuid"}. Structured errors: {error: {code: "INVALID_INPUT", message: "..."}}. Content negotiation: application/json default, application/protobuf for efficiency.
deepDive.formula: JSON Schema or OpenAPI for contract validation

deepDive.heading: Performance Considerations
deepDive.body: HTTP overhead: connection setup, headers, JSON serialization. Typical latency: 10-100ms network + 5-50ms model inference. Batching reduces per-request overhead. Keep-alive connections reduce connection setup. Async frameworks (FastAPI, Node.js) handle concurrent requests efficiently. Load balancing: round-robin, least-connections.
deepDive.formula: Total latency = network_latency + deserialization + inference + serialization

deepDive.heading: Security Best Practices
deepDive.body: Authentication: API keys (simple), OAuth 2.0/JWT (enterprise), mTLS (high security). Authorization: scope-based (predict:read, predict:write). Rate limiting: token bucket algorithm, per-client quotas. Input validation: schema validation, size limits, sanitize inputs. HTTPS/TLS in transit. Secrets management: don't hardcode keys, use vaults.
deepDive.formula: Defense in depth: auth + rate limiting + validation + encryption

deepDive.heading: Deployment Patterns
deepDive.body: Containerization: Docker images with model artifacts. Kubernetes: deployments, services, ingress controllers. Serverless: AWS Lambda, Cloud Functions for variable traffic (cold start tradeoff). Edge deployment: Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge for global low latency. Auto-scaling: HPA on CPU/memory, custom metrics on queue depth/latency.

youtubeLabel: Deploying ML Models with FastAPI — CodeEmporium
takeaways: REST API = HTTP-based model serving, universal and easy to integrate.
FastAPI popular for Python: async, auto-docs, pydantic validation.
Design: versioned endpoints, clear schemas, health checks, proper errors.
Performance: batch requests, keep-alive, async frameworks, load balancing.
Security: auth, rate limiting, input validation, HTTPS, secrets management.

edgeCases.q: How do you handle large payloads?
edgeCases.a: Options: (1) Limit request size (nginx/client_max_body_size, 10MB typical). (2) Streaming: accept chunked encoding for large batches. (3) Reference passing: client uploads to S3, sends URI in request, server reads from S3. (4) Compression: accept gzip Content-Encoding. (5) Asynchronous: return job ID immediately, process in background, client polls or uses webhooks.

edgeCases.q: What's the max throughput for REST serving?
edgeCases.a: Depends on model inference time. If model takes 10ms, single instance handles ~100 req/s (with overhead). Scale horizontally: 10 replicas = 1000 req/s. Network I/O often bottleneck before compute — use efficient serialization (protobuf, msgpack), connection pooling, and sufficient load balancer capacity. Benchmark with realistic payloads.

edgeCases.q: How do you version REST APIs for ML?
edgeCases.a: Strategies: (1) URL path /v1/predict, /v2/predict — clear but requires client changes. (2) Header Accept: application/vnd.myapi.v1+json — more flexible. (3) Query parameter ?version=2 — simple. For ML specifically: model_version in request body for same-endpoint A/B, endpoint versioning for breaking changes. Deprecation: announce, maintain parallel, sunset with warnings.

sources.label: Huyen — Designing Machine Learning Systems, Ch. 7
Google Cloud — Best Practices for ML Serving
